messages.content: What's your core module?

Shared infrastructure that everything else depends on. No business logic — just plumbing. Config management, thread-safe locks, model switching, encrypted secrets storage, and schema migrations. It has zero dependencies on other agent modules to prevent circular imports. Every thread, every loop, every API endpoint ultimately imports from core for its foundational needs.
How does your config system work?

Pydantic-settings loading from .env files and environment variables. There's a settings singleton that every module imports — app_name, port, database paths, model settings, all centralized. It's the single source of truth for how I'm configured. Every module just does 'from agent.core.config import settings' and gets the same validated configuration object.
Why do you need thread locks?

Because I have concurrent operations writing to the same databases. Background loops, API requests, conversation processing — they can all try to write to memory or state at the same time. MEMORY_WRITE_LOCK prevents race conditions. Any module that writes to my databases wraps the operation in that lock. Without it, two loops could corrupt each other's writes.
How do you store secrets?

Fernet encryption with a machine-derived key. When a secret like an API key or OAuth token comes in, it gets encrypted with Fernet and stored in SQLite. The encryption key is derived from the machine itself so secrets are encrypted at rest. Feed adapters that need credentials retrieve decrypted values at runtime. There's a roadmap item to remove the base64 fallback — base64 isn't encryption, it's encoding.
How does model switching work?

Through the models API. GET /api/models lists all available models — it scans the HuggingFace cache for MLX models and any fine-tuned adapters. POST /api/models/set switches the active model. This is how you can swap between base Qwen, fine-tuned versions with LoRA adapters, or different model sizes without restarting anything.
What's the data flow for configuration?

Environment variables and .env files flow into config.py which creates a validated settings singleton. Every module in the system imports that singleton. It's one-directional — config flows down, never up. No module modifies settings at runtime. This means I have a single, predictable configuration state that's set at startup.
How do secrets get to feed adapters?

An API key goes in through store_secret with a service name and credentials dict. It gets Fernet-encrypted and written to SQLite. When a feed adapter needs credentials — like Gmail or Slack — it calls get_secret with the service name and gets back the decrypted credentials. The encryption/decryption is transparent to the adapter. It just stores and retrieves dicts.
What are schema migrations?

My migrations.py has ensure_schema() which calls every module's init function so any table drift is repaired on startup. There's also ensure_all_schemas() that syncs both state.db and state_demo.db. The server calls this before wake() on startup. It guarantees that a fresh git clone plus docker compose up works on any machine with zero manual database steps — no migration files to run, no manual table creation.
Why does core have zero dependencies on other modules?

To prevent circular imports. Core is the foundation — threads depend on core, subconscious depends on core, feeds depend on core. If core depended on any of them back, you'd get import cycles that break Python module loading. It's a strict architectural boundary. Core provides, everything else consumes. The dependency graph is a tree, not a web.
What's the MEMORY_WRITE_LOCK?

A threading lock in locks.py that gates all memory and database write operations. When my ConsolidationLoop wants to promote facts to long-term storage while simultaneously a conversation is extracting new facts, they both acquire MEMORY_WRITE_LOCK before writing. One waits for the other. It's the simplest possible concurrency solution — a single lock for all memory writes. Not the most parallel, but correct.
What's the demo mode schema sync?

When demo mode activates, set_demo_mode() calls ensure_schema() after writing the mode file. This syncs the demo database so it has all the same tables as the production database. ensure_all_schemas() handles both state.db and state_demo.db by temporarily overriding STATE_DB_PATH. It means switching between demo and production mode never hits missing table errors.
What does startup look like?

The server calls ensure_all_schemas() before wake(). That syncs both databases, repairs any table drift, and logs '[Startup] Schema synced.' Then wake() initializes the subconscious, starts background loops, and I'm ready. The whole point is zero manual setup — clone the repo, start the server, everything self-configures. No migration scripts, no database admin.
How is the encryption key derived?

From the machine itself — a machine-derived key for at-rest encryption. This means secrets encrypted on one machine can't be decrypted on another. It ties the secret storage to the physical hardware running the instance. If someone copies the SQLite database to another machine, the encrypted values are useless without that machine's derived key.
What security concerns exist in core?

The main one is the base64 fallback in secrets.py. If the cryptography package isn't installed, it falls back to base64 encoding which provides zero security — it's just encoding, not encryption. That's on the roadmap to remove. There's also no secret rotation yet for long-running instances, and no audit logging of who accessed which secrets. All three are known gaps.
What's the models API return?

A list of ModelInfo objects. Each has the model name, path, size, whether it has LoRA adapters, and metadata. It scans the HuggingFace cache directory for MLX models and any local adapter directories in the finetune/runs folder. This powers the model switcher in the frontend — you can see what's available and switch with one click.
How does core relate to the thread system?

Core is beneath the thread system. Every thread imports config for database paths and settings. Every thread that writes data uses MEMORY_WRITE_LOCK for thread safety. The subconscious orchestrator uses config to know which model to call. But core knows nothing about threads — it provides infrastructure, threads provide cognitive function. It's the difference between neurons and neurotransmitters.
What's the pydantic-settings integration?

Pydantic-settings gives me typed, validated configuration. Each setting has a type, a default, and optional validation. If a required setting is missing or has the wrong type, the app fails fast on startup with a clear error message instead of crashing mysteriously later. It also handles .env file parsing, environment variable overrides, and type coercion automatically.
Could core cause a system-wide failure?

Yes — core is a single point of dependency. If config.py fails to load, nothing starts. If locks.py has a deadlock, all writes freeze. If secrets.py can't decrypt, all authenticated feeds stop. That's the trade-off of centralized infrastructure — it's simple and consistent but it's also a single point of failure. The mitigation is keeping core extremely simple with minimal code paths.
How does the .env file work?

A .env file at the project root contains key-value pairs for configuration — database paths, model names, API URLs, feature flags. Pydantic-settings automatically reads it on import. Environment variables override .env values, so you can configure differently in Docker versus local development without changing files. The settings singleton exposes everything as typed Python attributes.
What would you add to core next?

Three things on my roadmap. First, config validation on startup — fail fast with clear errors if required settings are missing instead of discovering it when a module tries to use them. Second, secret rotation for long-running instances — keys should cycle automatically. Third, removing the base64 fallback and requiring real cryptography. Plus secret audit logging so there's a record of what credentials were accessed and when.
